title: Cashback & Loyalty Tokens
introText: Test your knowledge of loyalty tokens, trust lines, and issuer verification on Stellar.

q1.question: What is a loyalty token?
q1.hint: Think about custom assets on the blockchain.
q1.a1: A custom asset on Stellar that a company issues as a reward.
q1.a1fb: Correct: Loyalty tokens are custom assets that companies create and issue themselves.
q1.a2fb: Wrong: XLM is Stellar's native currency. Loyalty tokens are independent custom assets.
q1.a3: A token issued by the Stellar Development Foundation.
q1.a3fb: Wrong: The SDF doesn't issue loyalty tokens. Each company creates its own.

q2.question: What does a customer need before receiving loyalty tokens?
q2.hint: Stellar requires explicit trust before a token can be received.
q2.a1: A trust line to the token's issuer.
q2.a1fb: Correct: Without a trust line to the issuer, no token can be received.
q2.a2fb: Wrong: Stellar accounts don't require external verification for custom assets.
q2.a3: At least 10 XLM in the wallet.
q2.a3fb: Wrong: There is no minimum XLM requirement for trust lines.

q3.question: Marco names his token "BERRY". Another company does the same. Are the tokens the same?
q3.hint: What distinguishes two tokens with an identical name on Stellar?
q3.a1: No – the issuer is different, so they are different tokens.
q3.a1fb: Correct: On Stellar, not just the name but name + issuer address uniquely identifies a token.
q3.a2: Yes – the same name means the same token.
q3.a2fb: Wrong: The same name doesn't mean the same token. The issuer is the decisive factor.
q3.a3fb: Wrong: Price is irrelevant. Two BERRY tokens with different issuers are fundamentally different.

q4.question: What determines the value of a loyalty token?
q4.hint: Who stands behind the token and decides its rules?
q4.a1: The issuer – they define what the token is worth and how it can be redeemed.
q4.a1fb: Correct: Without an issuer, a loyalty token has no value. They set redemption terms and supply.
q4.a2: The market price on the DEX.
q4.a2fb: Wrong: Loyalty tokens often have no DEX price. Their value comes from the issuer's promise.
q4.a3fb: Wrong: The SDF doesn't set rules for private loyalty tokens.

q5.question: You receive an offer of 50 free BERRY tokens via message. What is the safest first step?
q5.hint: Who is the actual creator of this token?
q5.a1: Compare the issuer address with the company's official address.
q5.a1fb: Correct: Only comparing the issuer address protects against worthless fake tokens.
q5.a2fb: Wrong: Adding a trust line to an unverified issuer can result in worthless tokens.
q5.a3: Search for the token name online.
q5.a3fb: Wrong: The name alone means nothing – anyone can name a token "BERRY".

q6.question: A token has the same name as a real loyalty token but a different issuer. What does that mean?
q6.hint: What makes a token unique on Stellar?
q6.a1: The token is worthless – without the right issuer it has no redemption value.
q6.a1fb: Correct: Name + issuer = unique token. Different issuer = different, worthless token.
q6.a2: It's the same thing, just from a different address.
q6.a2fb: Wrong: It is a fundamentally different token, not "the same from elsewhere".
q6.a3: It is safer because it is more decentralized.
q6.a3fb: Wrong: A different issuer doesn't make the token safer, it makes it meaningless.

q7.question: Can a company recall its loyalty token?
q7.hint: What flags can an issuer set when creating a token?
q7.a1: Yes, if the token was created with the clawback flag.
q7.a1fb: Correct: The clawback flag allows the issuer to reclaim tokens from recipients.
q7.a2: No, blockchain tokens are always permanent.
q7.a2fb: Wrong: With the clawback flag, tokens can be recalled. It's a deliberate design choice.
q7.a3: Only the Stellar Development Foundation can do that.
q7.a3fb: Wrong: Only the issuer itself can use clawback, not the SDF.
